articles.title: Himachal braces for hotter summer after dry winter - The Tribune
articles.search_term: heatwave (Bilaspur OR Chamba OR Hamirpur OR Kangra OR Kinnaur OR Kullu OR "Lahaul and Spiti" OR Mandi OR Shimla OR "Sirmaur (Sirmour)" OR Solan OR Una) Himachal Pradesh
articles.full_text:
Unlock Exclusive Insights with The Tribune Premium
Take your experience further with Premium access. Thought-provoking Opinions, Expert Analysis, In-depth Insights and other Member Only BenefitsAfter a largely dry winter, Himachal Pradesh is likely heading into a significantly hotter summer, with the State Meteorological Department projecting a rise in the number of heatwave days across the region. The forecast indicates a moderate probability, ranging between 35 and 65 per cent, that heatwave days will exceed the long-term average this season.
Temperature trends are also expected to remain elevated in the coming months. Both maximum and minimum temperatures are very likely to stay above normal in most parts of the state until June 2026. The department estimates a 45 to 75 per cent probability that minimum temperatures will remain above normal between April and June, pointing to warmer nights alongside hotter days.
In the high and mid-hill regions, maximum temperatures are expected to remain above normal with a probability of 45 to 65 per cent. Meanwhile, in the low hills and plains, there is a 45 to 65 per cent likelihood that maximum temperatures will hover around normal levels, suggesting some regional variation in heat intensity.
Rainfall patterns, however, present a mixed outlook. For April, there is a low to moderate probability, between 33.5 and 55 per cent, that many parts of the state could receive above-normal rainfall. In contrast, certain areas, particularly in Lahaul and Spiti and Kinnaur districts, may witness normal to below-normal precipitation during the same period.
The department has further highlighted a 55 to 75 per cent probability of above-normal minimum temperatures across most regions in April. Additionally, maximum temperatures are also likely to remain above normal in the majority of areas during the month, with a probability ranging from 45 to 75 per cent.
March rainfall data reflects the emerging trend of variability. The state recorded a 12 per cent deficit, receiving 99.6 mm rainfall against the normal of 113.4 mm. Among districts, Chamba emerged as the driest, registering a 33 per cent deficit with 119.7 mm rainfall compared to the normal 177.4 mm. On the other hand, Solan recorded the highest rainfall, with 99.6 mm against a normal of 61.9 mm, marking a 61 per cent surplus.
In the immediate term, the Meteorological Department has forecast heavy rain and snowfall activity across the state until April 7, with particularly intense weather expected on April 3 and 4. An orange warning has been issued for Shimla, Kullu, Kangra, Mandi and Chamba districts on these dates, while a yellow warning remains in place for several districts from April 3 to 5, indicating the likelihood of adverse weather conditions.
